---
title: "Quarto template"
subtitle: "Using the tidyverse and Quarto"
date: today
date-format: "dddd, D MMMM YYYY"
institute: "Université du Luxembourg"
author: "Aurélien Ginolhac, Roland Krause"
logo_url: "https://github.com/ginolhac/unilu-theme"
format:
  unilu-theme-revealjs:
    output-file: index.html
from: markdown+emoji
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-background-image: https://tidyverse.tidyverse.org/logo.png
  data-background-position: "50% 10%"
  data-background-size: "100px"
  data-background-color: "#142F3F" # required for title background
execute: 
  echo: true
---

## Content

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Aims
This presentation demonstrates typical use of the `unilu-theme` Quarto template for common use cases.

Not all elements of the templates of the Powerpoint slide deck are implemented. 

Notably, 
- the two-tone section slides proved hard to get right.
- the title slide is a lot more modest

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Remarks
The main focus are data science related courses teaching R and Python.
Font sizes are relatively small to place code and data on the same slide.
:::
::::


# Standard layouts {.chapter}
Typical layout situations

## Standard two column code {.no-logo}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Code
```{r}
options(width=60)
head(swiss)
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
### Plotting/Result
```{r}
plot(swiss$Agriculture, swiss$Education)
```

:::
::::

## Standard two column layout

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Subheader

Presentations are set to 1600 x 900 with small font sizes to maximize content display
for application in data science teaching 

:::
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}
### Boxes
::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
### Emphasize with callout boxes
Use sparingly - this is the **important** box. 
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### Emphasize with callout boxes
Default for callout should be **note** and **important** matching most closedly to primary uni colors - this is the **note** box. 
:::

:::
::::

## Standard layout

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Header best as level3 (h3)
Smaller headers (h4/h5/h6) are possible but h2 fill create new slide and break presention. 

:::
::: {.column width="50%" .highlight}
### Emphasis
A less intrusive way to highlight is the `.highlight` tag that will set this light red background.

:::
::::

## Practical {.slide-practical}

Using `{.slide-practical}` class in the header2

Absolute positioning of image and text, increment with `. . .`

::: {.absolute top="35%" left="15%"}
![](https://rworkshop.uni.lu/img/rstudio_blank.png)
:::

. . .

::: {.absolute bottom="10%" left="25%"}
::: {style="color: #FF0000"}
Console
:::
:::


## Logo link

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

Following discussion on [quarto-cli](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/discussions/3082#discussioncomment-7058099), logo on each slide can point to the desired URL.

Set it in the YAML header with `logo_url: "https://fasterthanli.me"`
:::
::: {.column width="50%" .highlight}

If not set, it points to https://quarto.org
:::
::::




# Quarto tips and tricks {.chapter}

## First slide

::: {.incremental}
- `{.incremental}` fenced div :+1:
- `from: markdown+emoji` in header allows emoji usage :smile:
- Chunk options are `hashpipe`, completion works great :wave:
- Great trick in RStudio: `CTRL`+[`,`]{.huge} :heavy_check_mark:
:::

. . .

`echo: fenced` ideal for teaching

- Fragment for **increment**


```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| output-location: column-fragment
1 + 3
mean(c(1, 3, 6))
```



## Columns as fenced divs `{.columns}` and LaTex {.smaller}


Trying to get rid of [xaringanExtra](https://pkg.garrickadenbuie.com/xaringanExtra/#/)^[Developed by [gabenbuie](https://github.com/gadenbuie)]

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
contents... 40%
```{=tex}
\begin{gather*}
a_1=b_1+c_1\\
a_2=b_2+c_2-d_2+e_2
\end{gather*}
```

:::

::: {.column width="60%"}

Playing with ` {.fragment}` transitions

::: {.fragment .highlight-red}
Highlight red
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
Fade in
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-up}
Slide up while fading in
:::

:::

::::

## YAML

`::: {.panel-tabset}`

::: {.panel-tabset}

## yaml

```
---
format: test
---
```

## Or nothing

:::


## Layout for images

::: {layout-ncol=2}

![Install `dplyr` via RStudio](https://i.imgur.com/hziKGjp.png){.center}

![Install using the console](https://i.imgur.com/RUzyzkw.png)

[Two ways of installing **packages**]{.center}

:::


## `.r-fit-text` class


::: {.r-fit-text}
Big Text
:::

More in the official [Quarto Presentation demo](https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs/demo/#/title-slide) and available [code](https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-web/blob/main/docs/presentations/revealjs/demo/index.qmd)

## Blockquote

::: {.huge}
>**Literate programming** is the practice of [mixing]{.red} code and descriptive writing in order to execute and explain a [data analysis]{.red} **simultaneously in the same document**.


— _William Landau_
:::

::: {.footer}
Source: [`{targets}` user manual](https://books.ropensci.org/targets/literate-programming.html)
:::


## Layout for chunks

- Highlight code lines, incremental with `|`
- Execute code _inside_ a caption with `!expr`

```{r}
#| echo: fenced
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| code-line-numbers: "|3-4,6|7"
#| fig-cap: !expr glue::glue("Swiss data are {nrow(swiss)} rows")
#| fig-height: 3
plot(swiss$Agriculture, swiss$Fertility)
plot(swiss$Agriculture, swiss$Education)
```



## Custom callout boxes

:::: {.columns}

### Defaults

::: {.column width="20%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
### Tip
Defaults callout boxes use the default icon quarto that comes with Quarto.
:::

:::

::: {.column width="20%"}

::: {.callout-note}
### Note
Callout boxes are not aligned. 
:::

:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
::: {.callout-important}
### Important default
It is usually not helpful to use more than one per slide
:::


:::

::: {.column width="20%"}

::: {.callout-caution}
### Caution
Use level 3 (h3, `###`) to set the title of the callout box. 
:::

:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
::: {.callout-warning}
### Warning default
Level 2 header create a deathloop with the presentation returning to the start.
:::
:::



::::


:::: {.columns}

### Custom icon and dark background
::: {.column width="20%"}
::: {.callout-tip  icon=false}
### {{< fa arrow-right >}} Tip
Use `icon=false` in callout div allows to set your own icon before the title. 
:::

:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
::: {.callout-note icon=false}
### Note
Works also without the icon. The icon-less variant uses darker background and white text and icon.
:::

:::


::: {.column width="20%"}

::: {.callout-important icon=false}
### {{< fa circle-exclamation >}} Important
With no icon, suggesting to use fontawesome icon
:::


:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
::: {.callout-caution icon=false}
### {{< fa road-barrier >}} Caution
Cats!

:::

:::

::: {.column width="20%"}
::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
### {{< fa triangle-exclamation >}} Warning
The end is near.
:::

:::

::::

## Callouts in two column layouts

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Data 

```{r}
head(swiss)
```

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
### {{< fa notes-medical >}} Note
Switzerland is mostly in the Alps.
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Elevation


```{r}
for (i in letters[1:3]){
  print(i)
}
```

::: {.callout-important}
### Important
Alps are mountains!
:::

:::
::::

# Code {.chapter}
Some text on section slide

## Python and R

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Python
```{.python}
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class DataSign:
  n = 3
  
  def f(self):
    for i in range(0, 3):
      if (i <= 10):
        print(i)

d = DataSign()

```

Python is using a different background for cases where R and Python code are displayed side by side.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### R
```{r}
mle_obj <- stats4::mle(function(x = 1) (x - 2) ^ 2)
typeof(mle_obj)
print("We don't write for loops")
```

:::
::::

## Snippets

Creating and editing slides can be much enhanced by using code snippets
 for column (standard layout).

## Customisations

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
It can be useful to adapt the style slightly if you're running multiple courses in parallel. 
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

:::
::::

## Before we stop

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Conclusion
More research is needed.
:::

::: {.column width="50%" .thanks}
Thank you for your attention!
:::
::::
